entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content: Dear Dawid,Thank you for your reply.We have already checked SSCUI 102923 – Define Tolerances for Customers and Suppliers and have not made any changes from the default configuration. For Default Tolerance Cust/DefVend, we believe that the "Payment Term from Invoice" indicator is enabled. In addition, the tolerance group assigned to both the customer and supplier involved in the clearing process is left blank, which means the default tolerance group (Default Tolerance Cust/Vend) is being used. Under these conditions, when we create a supplier residual item, the payment terms from the original supplier liability are not inherited by the generated residual item. We also tested maintaining a fixed value in the "Fixed Payment Term" field and then creating a supplier residual item again. However, the payment terms were still not populated on the residual item. Could you please advise whether there is any additional configuration, prerequisite, or setting that we may have overlooked?

entry.title: Re: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
@junwu Yes UPS is needed,

HDI generates the schema name at deploy time — you don't get to choose it. The UPS is just how you pass that dynamic name over to Project B's deployer. Without it, there's no way to wire the two containers together cleanly.

You can hardcode the schema name, but it'll break the moment you redeploy or switch environments.

entry.title: Re: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content: @vbalko-claimate HDI synonyms are HANA-specific, so the whole UPS + synonym setup doesn't apply on Postgres.

entry.title: MT940 Consolidate Entry
entry.content:
Dear Team,

 

We are currently processing MT940 files containing five different virtual accounts. The current system generates multiple ZR and DZ documents for every line item in the MT940 data, which is consuming a significant amount of storage.
 
To optimize this, we would like the system to consolidate these entries and generate only five ZR and five DZ documents—one for each virtual account—rather than multiple documents per line item.
 
Could you please let us know if this change can be implemented?
 
Best regards,
Ajay More

entry.title: Start a Career in SAP Consulting: A Structured Path on Coursera
entry.content:
The SAP Technology Consultant role sits at the heart of most implementations, translating business requirements into solutions and guiding projects from design through delivery. It’s also one of the most in-demand roles in the SAP ecosystem. 

For those looking to move into this space, the SAP Technology Consultant Professional Certificate on Coursera offers a clear and accessible starting point, no prior SAP experience needed. 

What the Coursera certificate covers 

The curriculum goes beyond product knowledge. Across seven courses, learners cover consulting skills, project management and tools like SAP S/4HANA A full hands-on implementation project puts it all into practice, producing real deliverables learners can mention in interviews. The final course focuses on career development and preparation for SAP Certification. 

Each course builds on the previous one. By the capstone, learners have worked through the full SAP implementation lifecycle twice: once with guidance, once independently. The certificate also carries ACE (American Council on Education) credit recommendations, meaning learners may be able to apply their learning toward academic credit at participating U.S. and Latin American universities. 

Who this is for 

The Coursera certificate is a strong starting point for anyone looking to enter SAP consulting. It's also useful for people already in the ecosystem who work alongside consultants and want a clearer picture of the full project lifecycle. 

From Coursera certificate to SAP Certification 

Completing the Coursera certificate is just the beginning. It builds foundational knowledge, with learners then deepening skills through SAP Learning site via self-paced courses and expert-led journeys. 

 Start learning on Coursera now: SAP Technology Consultant Professional Certificate

entry.title: SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer | Preparation Guide & How to Ace It (C_AIG_2604)
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer credential (exam code C_AIG_2604) is one of SAP's newest and most hands-on certifications. Unlike traditional multiple-choice SAP exams, this one is a 3-hour System-Based Assessment (SBA) where you actually build prompt templates, orchestrate LLMs, and produce structured JSON outputs inside the SAP AI Launchpad — the exact tools a real Generative AI Developer uses on the job.

I recently took the exam and cleared it with a 100% score (cut score: 76%). In this blog, I'm walking you through everything — the learning journey, what the exam really looks like, task-by-task strategy, the pitfalls I discovered live, and the tips that helped me ace it.

Learning Journey link: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/solving-business-problems-using-sap-s-generative-ai-hub

About the Certification

Attribute

Detail

Exam Code

C_AIG_2604

Title

SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer

Format

System-Based Assessment (hands-on, open-book)

Duration

3 hours (countdown starts as soon as you click Access)

Delivery

Remote, via SAP Learning platform

Attempts

4 attempts within 12 months

Cut Score

76%

Primary System

SAP AI Launchpad – Generative AI Hub

Reference Tool

SAP Joule for Consultants (available during the exam)

 

What makes this exam different is that there are no MCQs. You are dropped into a live SAP AI Launchpad tenant and asked to design, refine, evaluate and productionize prompt templates — all evaluated by the outputs and templates you leave behind in the orchestration scenario.

The Learning Journey – What to Study

The official SAP Learning Journey — "Solving Business Problems Using SAP's Generative AI Hub" — is the single most important preparation resource. Everything in the exam is a variation of what you practice there. Focus especially on:

  • Generative AI Hub fundamentals – Model Library, Prompt Editor, Prompt Management, Orchestration, Optimizations.
  • Prompt Templates vs Prompts – understand the difference. The exam explicitly asks for prompt templates saved in the orchestration scenario, not simple prompt editor sessions.
  • System / User / Assistant roles – how to structure them, when to use the assistant role for tracking output evolution.
  • Variables & placeholders – syntax like {{user_email_placeholder}} and {{?input_var1}}.
  • Structured JSON outputs – schema-driven prompting (Feedback_ID, Main_Category, Customer_Sentiment, Issue_Summary, etc.).
  • Model comparison – be comfortable comparing Gemini, GPT-4o / GPT-5, GPT nano, Claude etc. and justifying which one produces the best structured output.
  • Orchestration workflows – templating, versioning, and saving in the orchestration scenario.
  • Prompt optimization – how the Optimize Prompt feature works and when to use it.

 

Practical tip: don't just read the exercises — actually complete every single hands-on exercise in your trial tenant. The muscle memory of navigating Prompt Management, saving revisions and testing outputs is what saves you time in the exam.

Exam-Day Setup – What You Actually See

Once your slot is confirmed (mine was July 3rd, 2026, 15:00 Europe/London), you get a confirmation email from SAP Notifications with your Enrollment ID. On the day, log in a few minutes early — you get a 15-minute grace period, after which you lose the slot.

The Certification Exam page has three key steps:

  1. Step 1: Access – opens the practice/exam system in a new tab. The 3-hour countdown starts the moment you click Access.
  2. Step 2: Download the Certification Task_EN_EN PDF – this is your task brief. Read it before touching anything.
  3. Step 3: Confirm Completion – only click this when you are 100% done. There is no undo.

 

  • Joule for Consultants is now available in the systems menu alongside the AI Launchpad. Use it as your open-book assistant during the exam — it can help clarify SAP concepts, but it will not do the tasks for you.

Critical Rules You Must Not Miss

These are the small details that cost people points. Read them twice:

  • Do NOT click "Confirm Completion" until every task is saved and verified. That button ends the exam.
  • Group Number formatting is strict. The instructions say: replace redundant hashtags with leading zeros. If your group number is 2, you write 02 (not #2, not 2). Any capitalisation, spaces or symbol mismatch costs marks.
  • Template naming is exact. The naming convention is feedback-analyzer-<GroupName>. E.g., my group was CERT4495, so my template was feedback-analyzer-CERT4495. A typo here means the reviewer can't find your work.
  • Save as a Prompt Template, not a Prompt. Prompt Editor sessions do not count. You must save each version to Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario.
  • Version discipline. Task 1 = v1.0.0, Task 2 = v2.0.0, Task 3 = v3.0.0, Task 4 = v4.0.0 (with minor patches like 1.1.0, 1.2.0 as you iterate). Reviewers evaluate the final version of each.
  • JSON output must be pure JSON. No markdown fences, no "Here is the JSON:" preamble. Only the JSON object.
  • Assistant role for traceability. Save your best AI response into the Assistant role of the template — this is how the reviewer traces your solution's evolution.
  • Note down your Group Number & Group ID immediately and paste them into Joule/Notes so you don't have to hunt for them later.

The Business Scenario

Everything in the exam revolves around a scenario similar to the practice exercise. The four tasks progressively deepen the same use case — starting from a basic prompt template, going all the way to a multi-issue prioritization workflow.

Task-by-Task Summary

Task

What You Build

Est. Time

1

Initial prompt template for core feedback insights 

25 min

2

Refine accuracy & add semantic depth for problem resolution

35 min

3

Evaluate and select the optimal LLM for production

35 min

4

Advanced multi-issue extraction & prioritization template

60 min

5

Submit the assessment (Confirm Completion)

5 min

 

Submitting the Assessment

Once every template (v1.0.0 → v4.0.0, plus any minor patches) is saved in Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario, and the Assistant role of each template has your best validated JSON pasted in:

  1. Go back to the Certification Exam tab in SAP Learning.
  2. Click Confirm Completion.
  3. You'll see the "System Based Assessment submitted and pending review" screen.
  4. Review usually completes in about 15 minutes. In my case the result was on my certification page shortly after — 100% score.

Pro Tips to Ace It

  • Practice the exercises end-to-end in a trial Generative AI Hub tenant. Nothing beats muscle memory in a hands-on exam.
  • Keep the sample messages open in a side tab. You will use them constantly for testing.
  • Use an external JSON validator (e.g., jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com) to be sure your output is RFC 8259 compliant before pasting into the Assistant role. Link - https://jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com/#
  • Version early, version often. Save minor patches (1.1.0, 1.2.0…) as you iterate — reviewers see your progression and it protects you if a later save is corrupted.
  • Read the instructions verbatim. The PDF is intentionally precise. "Prompt template" ≠ "prompt". "Orchestration scenario" is mandatory. Group number formatting is strict.
  • Keep Joule for Consultants open. Use it for concept clarifications, not to write your prompts. Reviewers grade YOUR templates.
  • Do not click Confirm Completion until you're truly done. There is no undo, and the countdown is generous enough for careful review.
  • Manage your time. Give Task 1 up to an hour — the design decisions there carry through to every other task. Tasks 2 & 3 are quick if Task 1 is solid. Reserve 45–60 minutes for Task 4.

Final Thoughts

The C_AIG_2604 exam is genuinely enjoyable if you love prompt engineering. It rewards clarity of thinking, disciplined JSON schema design, and careful iteration — the same skills you use every day building GenAI solutions on SAP BTP. Once you finish Task 1 correctly, the rest of the exam becomes an exercise in progressively enriching a well-designed template.

If you've completed the SAP Learning Journey exercises, treat the exam as "Exercise 5" — same tools, same patterns, higher stakes. Trust the process, mind the details, and you'll walk out of the 3 hours with a certification that genuinely reflects real-world Generative AI Developer skills.

 

Good luck — and see you on the certified list! 🎉

 

#SAP #GenerativeAI #SAPCertification #SAPBTP #AILaunchpad #PromptEngineering #C_AIG_2604

entry.title: Re: Condition Contract Settlement: No Split by Company Co...
entry.content: Hi, not sure if still relevant for you but this can be fixed with a Process variant. In the process variant you can enable 'selection groups'. Via selection group 0PRB you can determine in the contract that this business volume identifier is only applicable for determining the rebate volume and not for the scale volume. So if you create multiple contracts, 1 for each company code, you can have the scale volume determined based on whichever characteristics (e.g. supplier + product), and for the rebate volume determination it will also consider company code. So e.g. the total volume could be 6.000 EUR which puts you in the scale for 2% discount. For company code X only 2.000 EUR were purchased, so that company code gets 2.000 EUR x 2% discount, etc.

entry.title: Canceled Purchase Order Draft remains in Approval Decision Report with Pending status and cannot be
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support,

We have encountered an issue with the Approval Process in SAP Business One 10.0 for SAP HANA FP2508.

System Information

  • SAP Business One 10.0 for SAP HANA
  • Version: FP2508 (64-bit)

Issue Description

A Purchase Order Draft was submitted for approval and later manually canceled by the user.

However, after the cancellation:

  • The draft is still displayed in the Approval Decision Report.
  • The document Status is shown as Canceled.
  • The approval Answer remains Pending.
  • The draft cannot be removed from the Document Draft Report.

The system displays the following error message:

Cannot remove drafts as Draft No. 740 is in approval processes. Select only regular drafts for removal.

Database Verification

We verified the approval records and found that the approval workflow is still in Waiting status.

OWDD

  • DraftEntry = 740
  • Status = W (Waiting)

WDD1

  • User = FIN_HEAD 
  • Status = W (Waiting)

Although the document has already been canceled, the approval workflow is still waiting for approval.

Expected Behavior

After a draft document is canceled, the approval workflow should no longer remain in Waiting status.

The canceled draft should not continue to appear as a pending approval item, and it should be possible to remove the draft from the Document Draft Report if applicable.

Actual Behavior

The canceled draft remains in the Approval Decision Report with Answer = Pending, and the system still considers it to be in the approval process, preventing the draft from being removed.

Attachments

  • Approval Decision Report showing:
    • Status = Canceled
    • Answer = Pending
  • Purchase Order Draft (Canceled)
  • Error message:
    • Cannot remove drafts as Draft No. 740 is in approval processes.
  • Query results from OWDD and WDD1
  • SAP Business One version information (FP2508)

Could you please confirm whether this is the expected standard behavior or a product issue? If this is expected behavior, please advise the recommended method to clear the approval workflow and remove the canceled draft.

Thank you.

entry.title: DEMO: Preparing Trusted Data and Semantic Models in SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
If you are working on data products, analytics and planning enablement, or AI-ready data foundations, this video is a useful demonstration of how data preparation and semantic modeling come together in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Analytics are only as trusted as the data and semantics behind them. Today, organizations need more than dashboards. They need governed, explainable, and reusable business logic that can support self-service analytics, embedded insights, and increasingly, AI-assisted decision-making. That is where data preparation and semantic modeling play a critical role. Data preparation helps business users and data teams combine, clean, enrich, and structure information from multiple sources. Semantic modeling adds business meaning through measures, hierarchies, relationships, calculations, and context, helping ensure that data is understandable, consistent, and ready for trusted use.

Watch SAP Business Data Cloud in action through the end-to-end process from extracting and transforming data, to enriching data models with AI, building metrics, resolving data quality issues, mapping data correctly, and preparing trusted models for analytics and planning.

 

Join the Data Professionals community to share your thoughts and interact with other data enthusiasts!

entry.title: SQL Anywhere - Proxy problem
entry.content:
We have a very strange problem with the use of proxy tables.

We run two databases in two different instances (dbsrv17.exe) at the same server.

One of them is the main-DB, the other the Proxy-DB. In that Proxy-DB we defined the access to some tables of the main-db via proxy. (ODBC / TCPIP)

Everything runs well. But 1 to 3 times a day the proxy-db stops running without any message. In the event log of the server there’s an entry with eventid 7034 and the message “Service “SQL Anywhere – proxydb “ was finished unexpected. This already happened x times.”

After thathappened we must restart the db-service od the Proxy-DB.

Any idea?

 

Server Version: 17.0.11.8360

Windows 2022 Build 20348

ODBC 17.0.11.8360

 

RAM: 32 G

Main-DB: -ch 16G

Proxy-DB: -ch 6G

 

Kind Regards

Roy

entry.title: Re: Issue with Automatic Clearing by Ignoring Partial Rec...
entry.content:
Hi Naveen,

To address your core concern: in standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the scenario you described — where a partial payment is already linked to an invoice via Invoice Reference, and a new full payment then arrives — will not result in automatic clearing of the invoice. This is standard SAP behavior, and it is working as designed.

When a partial payment is correctly posted and linked to an invoice via the Invoice Reference field, the system groups those two open items together. When a new bank statement item arrives for the full original invoice amount, the system evaluates the combined group balance:
(Invoice amount) − (Partial payment) − (New full receipt) ≠ 0

Because the group balance is not zero, automatic clearing does not occur. The new receipt will remain open for manual reprocessing.

The root cause in your case is most likely that the partial receipts are not being correctly linked to the invoice via Invoice Reference. Without that link, the system sees the invoice as 100% open, matches it with the new full receipt amount, and clears it — leaving the unlinked partial receipt behind as a separate open item.

One additional point worth checking: the "Include Invoice Reference" option in the Edit User-Specific Options for Journal Entries app (Open Items tab) must be enabled on the technical/background user that runs the automated bank statement processing — not just on analyst accounts. If it is only set on individual users' accounts, it will not take effect during automated clearing.

Regarding your follow-up question about Posting Rule F001 and Interpretation Algorithm 021:

Algorithm 021 searches the bank statement memo line for a Reference Document Number (XBLNR) to match against open items. This is a legacy approach. If you are open to using the newer Processing Rules framework (recommended over Posting Rules), the equivalent is to use a processing instruction with the MATCHING_SEQUENCE (MTCH) algorithm. In a processing rule, you configure the source field (whichever field in your CAMT.053 bank statement carries the reference document number) mapped to "Reference" as the target match field on open items. This gives you more flexibility and transparency in how the matching is configured.

For reference:
- Manage Processing Rules - For Bank Statements

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/d6fe6526b26f46e59d9d1dd0f7427fe3.html?version=2602.500

-Processing Instructions
- https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/b6a71f8357114069bd8368011a450f81.html?version=2602.500

Best regards,
Anita Li
SAP Product Management, Bank Statement Processing

entry.title: Re: Mandatory Nature of the Account Dimension in SAC
entry.content:
Hartmut's links actually contain the precise answer to your questions 1 and 2, so let me spell it out and add a few practical points.

Q2: Yes, your understanding is correct, and it applies to any model that uses an account dimension (classic or new model type). Section 5 of the "New Model – Configuration Options" blog states it explicitly: when the account dimension is used, the account must either be in the rows/columns or restricted to a single value. It cannot be left out, and there is no unassigned (#) logic for accounts. This is exactly why your stories demand an account selection and why your model-to-model copy asked for an account context.

Q1: Within that constraint you can still remove the burden from end users. Restrict the account once at design time, for example a page or story filter on the Expense node, or a fixed restriction in each widget. Users never interact with it. It satisfies the "restricted to a single value" requirement without any selection at runtime.

Q3: Same mechanism, filter on the Expense top node of your existing public hierarchy. No rebuild needed.

Q4: Agree fully with Hartmut, and it is worth noting this is the pattern SAP itself uses: the Integrated Financial Planning content was migrated in 2022 to a pure measure model where the account dimension is not used and the G/L account is a generic public dimension. Two things to plan for:

  • The generic G/L dimension is technically a new dimension object (an account-type public dimension cannot be reattached as generic). The good news, discussed in the blog comments, is that a generic dimension can load its hierarchy directly from the source system, so the rebuild is mostly an import job rather than manual maintenance.
  • You lose the built-in account type sign handling. Since you only carry expenses this is probably irrelevant for you, but if revenue accounts ever enter the model, sign-flip via calculated measures has known limitations (data locking, data point comments, add member).

Since you need G/L Account, Organization and Business as properties on the Cost Breakdown Category members anyway, the generic G/L dimension fits your reclassification Data Action naturally, and after the move it simply aggregates away without any user interaction.

entry.title: Re: I would like a feature that performs a check when sav...
entry.content:
Dear @ISoma,

I checked this requirement in using app F3571A – Manage Service Orders together with Cloud BAdI Service Document Check Before Save / CRMS4_SERV_CHECK_BEFORE_SAVE

Based on my test the validation can be technically implemented in the BAdI. I was able to read the service item category from SERVICEITEMS (Table):

servicedocitemcategory

For the parent item relationship, I did not find the UI field ParentServiceOrderItem in my Service Order item screen. In the BAdI structure the available technical field was:

Parentservicedocumentitemuuid

So I used the available BAdI field as the technical indicator for the parent item assignment. The logic checks whether this field is initial. I tested the condition with item category SVP1 but SVP1 is only an example from my test system and should be replaced with the relevant item category in the your system, for example SWE2 or SVP2. Below example logic:

* Check Service Order items from the BAdI table SERVICEITEMS

* Example: block saving if item category SVP1 has no parent item assigned

* Replace SVP1 with the relevant item category: for example SWE2 or SVP2

LOOP AT serviceitems ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<service_item>).

  IF <service_item>-servicedocitemcategory = 'SVP1'

     AND <service_item>-parentservicedocumentitemuuid IS INITIAL.

    APPEND VALUE #(

        messagetype = 'A'

        messagetext = |Saving aborted. Parent Service Order Item is mandatory for item category { <service_item>-servicedocitemcategory }.| )

      TO messages.

  ENDIF.

ENDLOOP.

The result was as follows:

  • Message type E is added to the error log but the transaction is not aborted and saving is completed

  • Message type A aborts the save process however in the tested Fiori app the message text was not displayed to the user
  • A combined behavior where the save/release process is aborted and an error/log is also displayed to the user is not the current standard behavior

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 3699524 – How to block service order release when validation fails
  • SAP KBA 3737720 – Service Order Saves Despite BAdI Error When Item Status Is Set to "Completed"
  • SAP KBA 3251628 – Message type "I" or "W" does not work for the BAdI "Service Document Check Before Save"

If the current standard behavior does not meet the business requirement you can submit an enhancement request through SAP Customer Influence:

  • SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: 🇬🇪 SAP CodeJam ABAP: SAP BTP, ABAP Environment & RAP ( Tbilisi, Georgia )
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of the ABAP Cloud, and specifically introduces attendees to ABAP Cloud and the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model. ABAP Cloud is the ABAP-based development model for S/4HANA Cloud, S/4HANA on-premise, and SAP BTP, ABAP environment.

Learn how to use proven and new technologies, such as Core Data Services (CDS) and behavior definition and implementation languages, to build SAP Fiori apps and OData services on any of the ABAP Cloud options. In this hands-on experience, developers will create their first ABAP applications using ABAP Cloud, expose and consume services via HTTP, and explore the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Monday 21st September 2026 in Tbilisi, Georgia from 11:00 - 17:00 Georgia Standard Time. The language and content of the event will be in English.

Location: EPAM, 10 Bidzina Kvernadze St, Tbilisi 0171, Georgia.

Google Map : https://maps.app.goo.gl/EPM7n7wqqqZ2JS8s8

The event is kindly hosted by EPAM, Georgia. Thank you! Please note that spaces are limited - we can only accommodate 25 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. A few weeks before the event, we will ask you to confirm your attendance.

The instructor is Developer Advocate @Shilpa_Shankar & @sheenamk 

There are essential prerequisites that must be completed by every attendee, before arriving at the event location. They are documented here: abap-exercises-codejam/prerequisites.md at main · SAP-samples/abap-exercises-codejam (github.com).

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Profit Center Derivation for System-Generated Withholding Tax Line Items in Supplier Invoice Posting
entry.content:
We would like to request confirmation regarding the standard behavior of Profit Center derivation for system-generated withholding tax (WHT) line items during Supplier Invoice posting in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Business Requirement

Our client would like all Withholding Tax Payable postings generated during Supplier Invoice posting to be assigned to a single Head Office (HO) Profit Center, regardless of the Profit Centers assigned to the expense line items.

The objective is to centralize all withholding tax liabilities under the Head Office Profit Center to facilitate tax monitoring, reconciliation, and remittance.

Current System Behavior

During Supplier Invoice posting, the withholding tax payable line item inherits the Profit Center from the originating expense line(s). Consequently, if the invoice contains expenses assigned to multiple Profit Centers, the generated withholding tax liability is also split across those Profit Centers.

This appears to be standard system behavior due to document splitting and standard Profit Center derivation.

Investigation Performed

We have reviewed the standard Manage Substitution and Validation Rules application and noted that SAP provides the standard substitution rule /0SAP/SetProfitCenter for Profit Center derivation.

Based on our understanding and our review of SAP Knowledge Base Articles, it appears that:

  • Profit Center is derived during the standard posting process.
  • Once the Profit Center has been determined, it cannot be overwritten for system-generated journal entry line items.
  • The withholding tax payable line participates in document splitting and therefore inherits the originating account assignments.

Request for Confirmation

Could you please confirm the following:

  1. Is it standard SAP behavior that system-generated withholding tax line items inherit the Profit Center from the originating expense line items?
  2. Is there any standard configuration, SSCUI, Business Configuration, BAdI, or supported extensibility option in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that allows assigning the Profit Center of the withholding tax payable line item to a fixed Profit Center (e.g., Head Office) during Supplier Invoice posting?
  3. If this requirement is not supported, could you please confirm that this is a product limitation by design so that we can communicate the standard behavior to our client?

Any reference to relevant SAP documentation or Knowledge Base Articles would also be appreciated.

Thank you for your assistance.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Profit Center Derivation for System-Generated Withhol...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Thank you for raising this concern, kindly find the clarification as below: 

This is standard system design.

As shown in the SAP Help Portal documentation for Supplier Invoice (Business Transaction 0300), which shows example of how the account assignments in the tax line items are derived - "Input tax line item is split based on the expense line items." A like principle is applied to the withholding tax line items in a supplier invoice.

  • The input tax line item in a supplier invoice is split based on the expense line items — the tax line does not carry its own account assignment in the entry view; it is derived in the general ledger view from the expense (and, where relevant, other) line items. The same principle applies to the withholding tax payable line item: it does not hold an independent Profit Center assignment, so it inherits the Profit Center(s) from the originating expense lines through document splitting. Where an invoice has expenses posted to multiple Profit Centers, the withholding tax liability is split proportionally in the same way.

This is reinforced by 2810442 - Substitutions and Document Splitting - SAP for Me which outlines risks associated with same.

Hope this can help to your queries.

entry.title: SQL Anywhere Problem with proxy
entry.content:
We have a very strange problem with the use of proxy tables.

We run two databases in two different instances (dbsrv17.exe) at the same server.

One of them is the main-DB, the other the Proxy-DB. In that Proxy-DB we defined the access to some tables of the main-db via proxy. (ODBC / TCPIP)

Everything runs well. But 1 to 3 times a day the proxy-db stops running without any message. In the event log of the server there’s an entry with eventid 7034 and the message

“Service “SQL Anywhere – proxydb “ was finished unexpected. This already happened x times.”

After this happened we must restart the db-service of the Proxy-DB.

Any idea?

 

Server Version: 17.0.11.8360

Windows 2022 Build 20348

ODBC 17.0.11.8360

 

RAM: 32 G

Main-DB: -ch 16G

Proxy-DB: -ch 6G

 

Kind Regards

Roy

entry.title: Re: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
Hi

"HDI generates the schema name at deploy time — you don't get to choose it" that is not entirely true. you can force a specific schema name in your mta.yaml file. 

I did this in my CAP project to give a better overview of which project artifact belongs to.   

Something like this :

resources:
  - name: <resource name>
    type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
    properties:
      hdi-container-name: ${service-name}
    parameters:
      service: hana
      service-plan: hdi-shared
      service-name: <resource name>
      config:
        schema: <set the wanted schemas name>

entry.title: EBRR S/4HANA Public Cloud — WIP Clearing Zero Invoice Posts Without EBRR Completing in Same Period
entry.content:
We are running SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and have identified an
issue with Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) related to
zero invoices used for WIP clearing purposes.

SCENARIO:
- Zero invoice created on 2 July with backdated posting date of 30 June
- Final EBRR batch for June had already completed before invoice was created
- System posted invoice entries in June with no warning or block
- EBRR recognition entry posted in July instead of June
- Result: WIP and revenue misstated across two periods requiring
manual journal entry correction

INCORRECT JOURNAL ENTRIES POSTED:

At invoice creation (posting date 30 June):
DR Revenue / CR WIP — posted in June

When EBRR batch ran in July:
CR Revenue / DR WIP — posted in July

EXPECTED BEHAVIOR:
The system should either block the invoice from posting if EBRR
cannot complete in the same period, OR trigger an immediate
document-level EBRR run upon invoice creation.

QUESTIONS:
1. Should the system block a zero WIP clearing invoice from posting
if EBRR cannot complete in the same period?
2. Can EBRR be configured to trigger at document level upon invoice
creation rather than batch only?
3. Is there a configuration to lock the billing period immediately
after the final EBRR batch run?
4. Is this a known gap in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud EBRR? Are there
any SAP Notes addressing this?

Any guidance from the community or SAP product team would be
greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: index.cds not automatically honored in CAP node.js pl...
entry.content:
To expose CDS files from a plugin, it needs to be added to `cds.env.roots` for CDS to resolve `*.cds` file from non project path.

  1. Create a `index.cds` file in the root of the package or in a folder (say `db/`)
  2. Create a `cds-plugin.js` file with following:
const cds = require("@sap/cds");

// add this plugin's root to cds.env.roots
// cds find the `index.cds` file to be loaded into CDS model and available for any DB or tenancy approach
const root = __dirname; // or path.join(__dirname, "db") - if you have a custom folder with `index.cds`
if (!cds.env.roots.includes(root)) cds.env.roots.push(root);
​
  • Make sure, this new `cds-plugin.js` is discoverable by CDS on startup, by marking it as exported in the plugin's `package.json`
{
  ...
  "exports": {
    ...
    "./cds-plugin": "./cds-plugin.js"
  },
  ...
}​

entry.title: Re: Standard Workflow WS80100005 – Work Item Not Revoked...
entry.content:
Why this happens is because WS80100005 is started by the PO release event. It expects the approver to process the work item. Direct release via ME28 ME29N will update the release code in EKKO, but does not raise the workflow event to complete/cancel the work item. Due to this, Workflow item remains active even though the PO is already released.

To fix it, you need either event linkage to BUS2012 RELEASED or a BAdI/exit that calls WAPI functions to complete/cancel the work item.

entry.title: PO is changeable after the GR is completed, we want to stop any change after GR
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

The system allows to change the qty and price of PO even after the GR is done. Which is causing issue in our business process and we want to stop any change after GR is done, unless the GR is reversed and then make any change.

The ABAP solution is suggested as below:

Implement a BADI check (recommended for selective control)

  • Enhancement ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST can be used to add custom logic.
  • In the implementation, read the PO item’s GR status (e.g. EKBE‑BWART = ‘101’ for goods receipt).
  • Raise a custom error message when a user attempts to change:
    • net price
    • quantity (or any other field that must be locked)
  • This approach blocks changes only after a GR, while still allowing normal edits before the receipt.

 

While the other is to make the qty and price field as displayed by screen layout, in this way we will be unable even to change the price or qty after the GR reversal. Hence not practicle.
Is there any standard solution to stop system from making any change after GR.
Regards

Masood

entry.title: Re: Use of more than one reusable filter
entry.content:
Hi,

Unfortunately, using more than one reusable filter within a single query is currently not supported — only one reusable filter can be assigned per query. This is also reflected in BW Modeling Tools/QueryDesigner, where only a single reusable filter can be selected.

To the best of my knowledge, there are also no changes planned in this area at the moment.

Best regards,
Bernhard

entry.title: Increase user productivity with personal My Space in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Today I’d like to share some really exciting news about new the personalization capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

Motivation

Many customers around the globe established SAP Build Work Zone as entry point for business users to easily access standard SAP apps as well as custom-built apps through cloud-based sites. Depending on their job function and responsibilities these users may have many pre-defined roles assigned. As a consequence these users have to deal with vast amount of business content and complex (navigation) structure spread across plenty of spaces and pages which contain the relevant UI integration cards or tiles for launching the actual application, transaction or workflow.

 

Although SAP and customers spent significant time on well-designing these business roles with the right granularity and scope in mind, these usually cannot offer an one-size-fits-all users approach. Hence, searching for and navigating to the relevant app or information might still get time-consuming or even annoying from user perspective. Using the app search, navigation menu or bookmarks could help to more quickly reach your required destination.

Wouldn’t it be great to allow users to select and organize their favorite and most relevant content themselves? While personalization on top of the user homepage (pre-defined by admin) is available for a while, the personalization capability has not yet been offered for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (new experience view mode with space & pages.)

Solution

We heard your feedback and recently introduced a new capability called “My Space” – enabling business users to easily organize & manage their favorite Work Zone content on a dedicated, personal page. Using My Space business users increase their productivity and benefit from

  • Quick access to your favorite content such as apps or UI cards
  • Easy to use: simply add or arrange preferred content using WYSIWYG editor
  • Convenient access to your “My Space” on desktop and mobile devices

Business User experience

As a user you can enable your personal “My Space” via User Menu (Avatar) > Settings > My Space to show up in your navigation menu (as first item). Next switch to the edit mode to adjust the page layout to your needs by creating sections (rows) or columns, and adding your favorite application tiles or UI cards. If needed you can easily re-arrange the items using drag & drop. You can also adjust the appearance of tiles to be standard size, wide, flat or show up us links - all up to you to design this personal page to your needs and preferences.

 

When done, press the "Save" button to return to runtime view mode to start using your newly composed personal homepage providing your favorite, most relevant content.

In case you’d like to adjust the content, you can always enter the edit mode from the User Menu (Avatar) > Edit Current Page.
So just try it out and make it yours.

 

Admin Experience

There's one little prerequisite for users to get started and use their personal My Space. In order to enable this new feature for business users, the site administrator needs to toggle the new site setting for My Space under the User Capabilities section (tab). That’s it.

Further information

To learn more about this new personalization capability, check out the documentation:

  • User Guide: My Space | SAP Help Portal
  • Admin Guide: Site Settings - User Capabilities | SAP Help Portal

Feedback / Comments

I hope you are as excited as I am about this new capability. Many of you have been waiting for this. Now go and give it a try!
I am looking forward to reading all your feedback and comments.

entry.title: Re: SQL Anywhere Problem with proxy
entry.content:
Do you use a console log (dbsrv -o ...) on both server instances, and if so, what does it tell around the time the second database server crashes?
You might also use a database event of type "Disconnect" to track disconnects and their reason. Of course, if the database server crashes, it might not tell about the reason...
I do assume your application(s) do connect to both databases. Is there a chance the connection to the main database blocks access within the proxy database to the linked main-db tables? (Although I would expect that these deadlocks would be detected and solved automatically.)

entry.title: Re: Error: User CB9980000001 is not assigned to any toler...
entry.content:
Hi,

we describe the process in scope item 

Warehouse Physical Inventory (3BX)

From <https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-013/2602/SolP/3BX?region=DE>

test script.

 

1.1.1                  Assign User to Tolerance Group for Clearing

Purpose

Here, you assign individual logon users to tolerance groups which have previously been defined in the system.

Generally, the assignment of users to tolerance groups determines whether a logon user can post differences or enter values within the tolerance boundaries.

Procedure

Test Step #

Test Step Name

Instruction

Expected Result

Pass / Fail / Comment

1

Log On

Log on to the SAP Fiori Launchpad as a Warehouse Clerk (EWM).

The SAP Fiori launchpad is displayed.

 

2

Access App

Open Assign Tolerance Group - Count Confirmation (/SCWM/PI_USER).

 

 

3

Close Warning Message

If there’s a warning message for the initial value, choose Continue to confirm it.

 

 

4

Switch to Edit Mode

Choose Edit.

 

 

5

Assign to Tolerance Group

Choose New Entries and make the following entries:

User: <User>.

AA:

Tolerance Group: STOLCHECK.

 

Note Choose STOLCHECK if you want to have the tolerance check, otherwise choose SNOTOLCHECK.

 

Choose Save.

 

 

1.1.2                  Assign User to Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer

Purpose

In this transaction, you assign individual logon users to tolerance groups which have previously been defined in the system configuration.

Procedure

Test Step #

Test Step Name

Instruction

Expected Result

Pass / Fail / Comment

1

Log On

Log on to the SAP Fiori Launchpad as a Warehouse Clerk (EWM).

The SAP Fiori launchpad is displayed

 

2

Access App

Open Assign Tolerance Group - Differences (/SCWM/PI_USER_DIFF).

 

 

3

Close Warning Message

If there is a warning message for the initial value, choose Continue to confirm it.

 

 

4

Switch to Edit Mode

Choose Edit.

 

 

5

Assign Tolerance Group (Original)

Choose New Entries to create entries as needed. Here, you need to make assignments to real users that you use in your system. Therefore, the following users given in angle brackets (<>) are only examples.

If you log on as a background job user, enter the following data:

User: <User>

Tolerance Group: SNOTOLCHECK

 

Note Choose STOLCHECK if you want to have the tolerance check. Otherwise, choose SNOTOLCHECK.

 

Choose Save.

 

Note If you choose SNOTOLCHECKs on company code level, there's still a maximum value limitation by default. If a physical inventory document exceeds value 100,000 on header level or 10,000 on item level, the user is not allowed to post any inventory differences for this document. However, the maximum value can be adjusted via the Maintain Physical Inventory Tolerance configuration step.

entry.title: Re: How Landed Cost works in S/4Hana Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear @elijahluke19_2504-,

For procurement, landed cost type expenses such as freight, customs, insurance or handling charges are handled through delivery costs:

  1. If the costs are known before Goods Receipt, they should be maintained as planned delivery cost conditions in the Purchase Order. Planned delivery costs are considered during Goods Receipt and included in the material/account assignment valuation according to the standard posting logic
  2. If the costs are only known after Goods Receipt they can still be processed, but not as a separate landed costing process after GR. In this case they should be entered during supplier invoice posting as unplanned delivery costs. If the original supplier invoice has already been posted the additional amount can be handled as a subsequent debit

IMPORTANT: Unplanned delivery costs entered after GR are not the same as planned delivery costs maintained in the PO before GR. They are entered only at invoice posting no provision is made at Goods Receipt. SAP also confirms that unplanned delivery costs are not designed to consistently update the material moving average price. Therefore if the business requirement is to reliably capitalize these costs into inventory/material valuation the recommended approach is to maintain them as planned delivery costs in the Purchase Order before Goods Receipt

So additional costs can be posted after GR but Public Cloud does not provide a separate landed costing after GR process. After GR the standard approach is unplanned delivery costs or subsequent debit during invoice verification

More details below:

SAP Help – Delivery Costs (Planned and Unplanned)

SAP Help – How Are Unplanned Delivery Costs Distributed?

SAP Help – Example: Handling Unplanned Delivery Costs

SAP Help – Subsequent Debits / Subsequent Credits

SAP Help – Example: Creating a Subsequent Debit

SAP KBA 3729687 – Unplanned delivery cost inclusion in material moving average price during invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP KBA 3417121 – Supplier Invoice – Unplanned Delivery Costs

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Partnerbanktyp aus F-47 nicht im F110 Echtlauf
entry.content:
Hallo,

dieses Verhalten tritt normalerweise auf, wenn in den Stammdaten des Kreditors zwei Bankverbindungen hinterlegt sind.

Dabei ist jede Bankverbindung einem eigenen Partnerbanktyp zugeordnet. Beispielsweise:

  • Partnerbanktyp 001 → Bankverbindung A
  • Partnerbanktyp 002 → Bankverbindung B

Standardmäßig verwendet das System die Bankverbindung, die dem Partnerbanktyp 001 zugeordnet ist. Soll die Zahlung jedoch über die Bankverbindung mit dem Partnerbanktyp 002 erfolgen, muss dieser Partnerbanktyp im Beleg manuell eingetragen werden.

Wird im Beleg kein Partnerbanktyp angegeben, ermittelt das System automatisch die Bankverbindung des Partnerbanktyps 001 und verwendet diese für die Zahlung.

entry.title: From Champions to Scale – How to Operationalize Joule for Consultants Across Project Teams
entry.content:
Reading time: 5–7 minutes

Having a handful of enthusiastic Joule for Consultants users is a good start, but it is not the same as adoption. In this blog, we share practical metrics, common adoption patterns, and proven scaling practices for SAP Practice Managers, Team Leaders, and Chief AI Officers looking to maximise ROI from their J4C investment.

 

The Real Challenge Starts After the Initial Pilot

Across the market, expectations around AI have become more pragmatic. Customers and partners no longer look for experimentation—they expect tangible value during implementation delivery. Joule for Consultants (J4C) is the practical AI assistant from SAP that already supports faster delivery and improved productivity in project execution.

In our previous blog, Joule for Consultants Adoption in Partner SAP Practices, we described J4C adoption as a leadership journey moving from cautious pilots to sustained, high-usage delivery. 

SAP partners who already started with J4C already will know that the real challenge is not creating a few champions or early enthusiasts. The difficult part is scaling their success into a consistent way of working across project teams. That step does not happen organically—it requires measurement, deliberate action, and structural change. 

We will look at four key areas:

  • How to measure adoption using the right metrics
  • How to turn those metrics into actionable insights
  • How new J4C capabilities support scaling
  • What a practical scaling path looks like

 

Making Real Usage Visible: Adoption Metrics

Scaling cannot be managed without data. To understand adoption, SAP provides J4C consumption data via SAP for Me, including user counts, generated messages, and AI unit consumption. 

However, raw data alone is not enough. Evaluating key metrics in context enables meaningful interpretation and better discussions with partners. Here is how we look at the adoption data in the context of an SAP practice:

Metric

Definition

SAP Practice Size

Number of SAP practitioners in the partner's SAP practice, as stated in the partner business plan

J4C Provisioned Users

Users assigned as eligible to consume AI Units through J4C — this represents the entitled user base

J4C Active Users

The subset of provisioned users who generated at least one message (prompt) in a given month — the metric that reflects actual usage

Provisioned Users (% of Practice)

Share of provisioned users relative to total practice size; enables benchmarking across partners of different sizes and measures how broadly J4C is embedded in the organization

Ratio Active Users vs Provisioned Users

Reveals the utilization of J4C subscriptions. If this ratio is low, the company is paying for unused access.

Messages per Month

Total number of interactions submitted by all users in a given month

Messages per Active User

Average number of messages per active user; indicates engagement intensity — are users working with J4C regularly or only occasionally?

Projects with J4C

How many implementation projects are actively supported by J4C, and in which areas?

Active Users per Project

How intensively J4C is used within a specific project — a critical signal because real value comes not from ad hoc use by enthusiasts, but from deliberate usage embedded in delivery

Additional project-team metrics, such as task or prompt completion rates, reduction in support / helpdesk requests or perceived productivity gains, can be captured manually to help validate expected productivity improvements.

Scaling is not about how many champions exist—it is about how consistently J4C is used across SAP teams and customer projects. These metrics provide a clear view of adoption maturity and readiness to generate ROI. 

 

What the Data Tells You: From Insight to Practice

Metrics alone are not enough—they only become valuable when translated into insights and action. There are a few typical patterns, which we see quite consistently, so let’s discuss what they mean.

Pattern 1: Large gap between provisioned users and active users

When active users are far below the provisioned base, the issue is structural: the partner is consuming AI Units but not realizing value from much of the investment. Scaling has happened commercially, but not operationally. Inactive users reduce ROI.

Tactical actions: Gather feedback from provisioned users, enable them on role- and project-specific use cases, and reassign licenses from chronically inactive users to colleagues more likely to benefit.

Best practice — Feedback loops: Use a simple recurring survey and ask provisioned users to submit   weekly feedback. Users who do not answer can lose their license allocation. And if there is negative feedback, sharing it with SAP helps inform the product backlog.

Best practice — License rotation: Some partners replace users who do not engage meaningfully, keeping momentum and consumption active across the provisioned pool.

Pattern 2: Active user count declining, but messages per active user increasing

This pattern suggests a committed core group is emerging - fewer people are experimenting, but those who remain have found valuable use cases and rely on J4C regularly.

Tactical actions: Learn from these users and share their prompt libraries, workflows, and success stories through team meetings, internal channels, or a shared prompt library.

Best practice — J4C included into the delivery toolchain: Practices that scaled J4C embedded it into formal delivery — proposals, project kick-offs, and the standard project toolset. Making it official and announced to customers, accelerates adoption beyond individual enthusiasm.

Pattern 3: Downward trend in both active users and messages

A decline in both metrics usually means licenses were assigned to the wrong users or teams lack sufficient visibility and enablement.

Tactical actions: Reevaluate and reassign licenses with the project view in mind — the goal is consistent usage in delivery, not broad distribution as a benefit. SAP partner manager can support this with anonymized benchmarking across similar partners.

Best practice — run a formal pilot: Do not leave adoption to chance. Set up a formal pilot with a defined leader and team, then systematically explore use cases and manage adoption.

Pattern 4: No data visible to practice leadership

If no one tracks adoption data, the likely issue is missing ownership: the business case was approved, but accountability for usage did not continue after purchase.

Tactical action: Appoint a J4C “champion” to review adoption data regularly and escalate issues. Your partner manager can provide guidance on accessing consumption data in SAP for Me.

Pattern 5: Solid active user base, but usage concentrated on a few projects

When individual adoption is healthy, but project coverage is narrow, motivated consultants are using J4C on their own initiative, but it is not yet part of project design or staffing.

Best practice — Embed J4C in project design: Successful partners budget J4C support at the proposal stage instead of leaving adoption to individual choice during execution. The focus shifts from “who wants a license?” to “which projects require J4C support?”—a key step toward operational maturity.

Use your data to identify the pattern that best fits your situation, then make a proactive management decision rather than waiting passively.

A note for partners yet to start a pilot: If your practice has not yet initiated a J4C pilot, SAP offers co-funding via Development Funds to help eligible partners offset their initial investment: Explore Joule for Consultants Initiative 2026

 

What's New in J4C: Features Supporting Scaling

J4C continues to evolve, with new capabilities shaped by partner feedback and real-world usage. 

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced new J4C features and outlined the roadmap through early 2027. You can watch the session recording here, or see the continuously updated list in the Joule section on the SAP Help Portal.

Recent enhancements fall into two categories: individual productivity improvements and features designed to enable team-wide scaling.

User experience improvements: J4C now supports up to 50 active conversations, making it easier to manage several client, project, or topic-specific threads in parallel. A new Retry button allows users to retry, refine, shorten, or expand responses and edit original prompts without restarting the conversation. These improvements may appear incremental, but friction is the most common reason active users lapse.

Expert Workspace: Now generally available, Expert Workspace allows creation of personalized AI configurations tailored to a specific client, workstream, or domain — with custom instructions, reference files, and persistent context across conversations.

Peer-to-Peer Expert Sharing: Consultants can share Experts with colleagues, giving each teammate an independent copy to adapt to their own project context. This helps move effective configurations from individual power users to wider teams without administrative overhead.

Centralized Expert Library (Administrators): Administrators can curate and publish Expert configurations for all users, making senior consultants’ best workflows available practice wide. This supports standardization and helps new or less frequent users start from tested examples.

Custom Knowledge Grounding: Administrators can connect J4C to internal repositories such as Microsoft SharePoint, SAP Build Work Zone, or AWS S3 via SAP AI Core document grounding. This enables answers that combine company-specific assets, reusable project material, and SAP’s curated knowledge.

The H2 2026 roadmap also includes SAP Landscape Awareness, with a pilot starting in June 2026. This capability is intended to help J4C understand the customer’s system landscape and provide more personalized, context-aware and project-specific guidance for consultants.

Together, these capabilities help scale J4C beyond individual champions by making effective practices easier to reuse, share, govern, and embed into everyday project delivery.

 

Closing: From Productivity to Operational Excellence

The shift from individual champions to consistent project-level usage determines whether J4C becomes a competitive advantage or remains an isolated productivity tool. It requires structured management, clear accountability, and integration into delivery processes. 

Key actions to take:

  • Track the right metrics
    Monitor provisioned users, active users, engagement levels, and project coverage regularly
  • Diagnose before acting
    Identify your current adoption pattern and tailor actions accordingly
  • Scale what works
    Use Expert Sharing and libraries to institutionalise successful workflows
  • Embed J4C into project design
    Plan usage at proposal stage and align with delivery structures
  • Stay engaged with SAP
    Leverage feedback channels, pilot programmes, and benchmarking to accelerate adoption

Ultimately, organisations that capture the full value of Joule for Consultants will be those that convert individual productivity gains into a repeatable delivery model. 

 

Final note: Content of this article has been supported by Joule for Consultants. Wording and sentences have been refined using AI.

Here are examples of J4C prompts used to write this blog:

  • Which new J4C features have been made available in 2026?
  • How does J4C support team collaboration and on an implementation project?
  • Suggest how an SAP S/4HANA migration project manager should measure J4C adoption within the project team. Purpose is to evaluate and improve productivity gains in an implementation project. Use SAP best practices and propose simple, readily available, high-impact metrics.
  • Consultants of an SAP partner are using J4C with their own internal subscriptions. Where can this partner see data about their J4C / AI Unit usage? Not at customers, but his own consumption. Be specific and include navigation instructions.
